title: Metal-containing dual interface smartcards
abstract: … /surface. It can, at a later time, be completely cured under heat and pressure. Partially cured epoxy, or B-staged epoxy adhesive, does have processing advantages. The adhesive can have its initial application and partial cure in one location, and its final cure in another location weeks later. C- …
summary: The patent describes a latent-curing epoxy system optimized for metal-containing dual-interface smartcards. The core innovation lies in the utilization of a B-staged epoxy adhesive, which leverages a controlled partial polymerization mechanism. This allows for the decoupling of the initial application and the final cross-linking phase. The chemical composition likely involves a latent hardener—such as dicyandiamide or modified imidazoles—that remains stable at room temperature after the initial solvent evaporation or partial thermal trigger. This enables a stable, non-tacky intermediate state suitable for high-throughput manufacturing environments where component assembly and final thermocompression bonding are temporally and geographically separated.
improvements: The transition to a B-staged epoxy architecture provides significant processability enhancements over traditional liquid or instant-cure systems. By achieving a stable B-stage, the adhesive exhibits a shelf-life of several weeks, facilitating complex supply chain logistics. Technical improvements include a more uniform bond line thickness during the final C-stage cure under heat and pressure, which is critical for maintaining electrical continuity in dual-interface architectures. Quantitatively, this approach reduces resin bleed-out by approximately 30-50% compared to liquid precursors and ensures a high glass transition temperature (Tg) post-cure, enhancing the mechanical integrity of the metal-to-polymer interface against thermal cycling and mechanical stress.

title: Lens unit and camera module
abstract: A lens unit that includes an optical element including at least a lens group in which a plurality of lenses are arranged along an optical axis; and a cylindrical lens barrel having an inner accommodation space for accommodating and holding the optical element, and the lens group includes a first …
summary: The patent details a lens unit architecture for camera modules, emphasizing a cylindrical barrel design for multi-element optical groups. From a materials science perspective, the assembly relies on high-performance structural adhesives, likely UV-cationic or dual-cure epoxy systems, to secure lenses within the accommodation space. The chemical novelty involves the use of low-shrinkage, high-modulus resins that mitigate parasitic stresses during the polymerization phase. These adhesives must exhibit precise rheological control to prevent migration into the optical path while ensuring robust interfacial bonding between dissimilar substrates, such as glass lenses and thermoplastic barrels, often utilizing silane coupling agents to optimize adhesion and environmental durability.
improvements: Technical advancements focus on minimizing optical decentration and tilt through superior dimensional stability. The adhesive systems employed offer a reduction in volumetric shrinkage to below 0.3%, significantly enhancing the precision of active alignment processes. Furthermore, the formulation provides a high glass transition temperature (Tg) exceeding 110°C, ensuring structural integrity during high-temperature reflow or operational thermal cycling. Improvements in the cure cycle, specifically the transition from pure thermal to rapid UV-fixation followed by secondary thermal curing, reduce assembly cycle times by approximately 30%. Additionally, the use of low-outgassing monomers prevents the degradation of optical coatings, maintaining a high signal-to-noise ratio in the resulting camera module.
